refined_fr.refined_fr: CR-NGHE-005.1: The NGHE solution shall derive performance requirements from worksite mission context.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates three distinct obligations: (1) deriving requirements from mission context, (2) recording them, and (3) managing them over time. Atomicity requires separating these concerns. CR-NGHE-005.1 focuses on the derivation activity—the logical transformation from mission inputs to performance targets—which is solution-neutral and testable. Recording and management are separate concerns addressed in sub-FRs. The FR remains in the problem domain (what performance targets must be determined and from what inputs) without prescribing how derivation is performed (algorithm, tool, process).
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that given a defined worksite mission context (e.g., site layout, production goals, safety constraints), the system produces a documented set of performance targets (cycle time, energy efficiency, availability) that are traceable to those inputs and satisfy acceptance criteria.
sub_frs: CR-NGHE-005.1: The NGHE solution shall derive performance requirements from worksite mission context.
CR-NGHE-005.2: The NGHE solution shall record derived performance requirements in a persistent, retrievable format.
CR-NGHE-005.3: The NGHE solution shall maintain a history of performance requirement baselines and calibration updates.
CR-NGHE-005.4: The NGHE solution shall establish traceability between recorded performance requirements and their originating worksite mission inputs.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Establish a parameterized mission-context intake process that captures worksite operational profile (hazard class, duty cycle, environmental conditions, safety constraints) and maps each input to a corresponding performance target via a documented traceability matrix, with version control for baseline snapshots.

DP-B: Implement a rule-based inference engine that translates worksite safety plan directives and operational policies into quantified performance constraints, storing results in a structured requirement specification with audit trail.

DP-C: Design a two-stage derivation workflow: (1) extract mission context from worksite documentation (safety plan, operational procedures, hazard assessments); (2) apply a standardized performance-mapping template to convert each context element into measurable targets, with manual review gates and sign-off checkpoints.

DP-D: Create a federated data model where worksite mission context is stored as immutable baseline records, linked to derived performance targets via explicit traceability relations, enabling historical comparison and change impact analysis without re-derivation.

DP-E: Establish a hybrid human-in-the-loop process: automated parsing of worksite documents to extract context signals, followed by expert review and manual refinement of performance targets, with all decisions logged in a decision record repository.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-C: Design a two-stage derivation workflow: (1) extract mission context from worksite documentation (safety plan, operational procedures, hazard assessments); (2) apply a standardized performance-mapping template to convert each context element into measurable targets, with manual review gates and sign-off checkpoints.

potential_sources: Tight coupling between context extraction and performance mapping logic—mitigated by explicit two-stage separation and standardized template (decouples domain knowledge from extraction mechanics).
Dependency on worksite documentation format and completeness—managed by defining a canonical intake schema and validation rules upstream.

Manual review gates introduce human-process coupling—acceptable because FR explicitly requires traceability and acceptance criteria demand documented, auditable derivation; automation alone cannot satisfy accountability requirement.
coupling_check.notes: DP-C achieves functional independence by separating concerns (extraction vs. mapping) and introduces explicit control points (review gates) that align with the acceptance criterion for documented, traceable performance targets. The standardized template prevents ad-hoc derivation and supports reproducibility. Coupling to worksite documentation is unavoidable but is managed through schema definition and validation.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Worksite safety plans and operational policies are available in machine-readable or structured form (or can be reliably extracted via document parsing). RISK: If documents are unstructured or inconsistently formatted, extraction fidelity degrades. MITIGATION: Define a canonical intake checklist and require worksite stakeholders to provide structured inputs (e.g., hazard matrix, duty-cycle table).

ASSUMPTION: A standardized performance-mapping template can be defined that is general enough to cover diverse worksite mission contexts yet specific enough to ensure consistent, defensible derivations. RISK: Template may be too rigid for edge cases or too loose to prevent subjective interpretation. MITIGATION: Develop template iteratively with domain experts; include worked examples and decision trees for ambiguous cases.

ASSUMPTION: Manual review gates are feasible within project schedule and resource constraints. RISK: Review bottleneck delays performance target finalization. MITIGATION: Establish clear review criteria and SLAs; consider tiered review (automated checks first, expert review second).

ASSUMPTION: Traceability matrix and baseline history can be maintained without significant overhead. RISK: Version control and audit trail management become burdensome if not automated. MITIGATION: Integrate with existing configuration management and document control systems; use lightweight traceability tools.

ASSUMPTION: Worksite mission context remains stable or changes are managed through a formal change-control process. RISK: Uncontrolled context drift invalidates derived performance targets. MITIGATION: Establish a change-notification protocol and trigger re-derivation when context changes exceed defined thresholds.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR-NGHE-005.1 (refined): The NGHE solution shall provide a two-stage, documented derivation process that (1) systematically extracts mission context from worksite safety plans and operational policies using a canonical intake schema, and (2) applies a standardized performance-mapping template to convert each context element into measurable, traceable performance targets. The system shall maintain an immutable baseline record and audit trail of all derivations, support historical comparison of performance targets across calibration cycles, and enforce manual review and sign-off gates to ensure accountability and traceability. Success is measured by: (a) 100% traceability of each performance target to one or more worksite context inputs, (b) documented derivation rationale for each target, (c) version-controlled baseline snapshots with change history, and (d) completion of derivation within [X] business days of worksite context intake.

im_boundary.name: Safety Services
im_boundary.scope: Centralized module responsible for deriving, managing, and validating safety-critical performance targets from worksite documentation through structured workflow with review gates and sign-off checkpoints.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Safety Services outputs derived performance targets and measurable objectives to Tasking for mission planning and vehicle command generation.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Safety Services receives hazard assessments and safety plan data as input; outputs validated safety constraints and alert thresholds derived from performance mapping.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Safety Services may ingest worksite telemetry or operational context to enrich mission context extraction during stage (1).

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: DP-C is fundamentally a safety-driven derivation workflow. Stage (1) extraction of mission context from safety plans, operational procedures, and hazard assessments is native to Safety Services domain. Stage (2) performance-mapping template and review gates align with safety validation and sign-off responsibilities. Minimal diffusion: only outbound interfaces to Tasking (derived targets) and SafetyAlert (validated constraints) are clean and well-defined.

Poor fit: NGHE Solution is a top-level system container, not a functional module. DP-C would diffuse across multiple sub-components (safety, tasking, teleoperation). No clear ownership or cohesion at this level. Overly broad and lacks domain specificity for safety-critical derivation workflows.

Very poor fit: Vehicle module is responsible for platform control and execution, not safety requirement derivation or performance target mapping. DP-C requires access to worksite documentation, safety plans, and hazard assessments—none of which are Vehicle responsibilities. Severe diffusion: would require Vehicle to depend on external safety data sources and perform non-vehicle-centric logic.

Very poor fit: Teleoperation is responsible for remote operator control and situational awareness, not safety requirement derivation. DP-C involves structured document analysis, performance mapping templates, and formal review gates—orthogonal to teleoperation concerns. High diffusion: would require Teleoperation to manage safety workflows unrelated to operator interface.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Safety Services is the natural owner of DP-C because: (1) it owns the safety domain and has authoritative access to safety plans, operational procedures, and hazard assessments; (2) performance-mapping templates and review gates are safety validation mechanisms; (3) derived targets flow cleanly outbound to Tasking and SafetyAlert without requiring bidirectional coupling or cross-module duplication; (4) manual review and sign-off checkpoints are safety governance responsibilities.
containment_risks: Risk: If worksite documentation ingestion requires real-time telemetry fusion, Safety Services may need tighter coupling to Telemetry interface than currently assumed. Mitigation: Design Telemetry interface as asynchronous data feed with clear schema.

Risk: If performance-mapping templates are domain-specific (e.g., different for mining vs. construction), Safety Services may become a bottleneck for template customization. Mitigation: Implement template registry pattern to allow pluggable, validated templates without modifying core Safety Services logic.

Risk: Manual review gates imply human-in-the-loop workflow; if operators are remote (Teleoperation domain), coordination overhead may increase. Mitigation: Define clear escalation and approval workflows with explicit role assignments independent of Teleoperation module.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: HIGH. Acceptance criteria require documented performance targets traceable to worksite inputs and maintained history of baselines/calibration updates. Safety Services module boundary explicitly owns derivation workflow with review gates and sign-off checkpoints, enabling audit trail and traceability. Telemetry interface provides worksite context ingestion; outbound Tasking interface enables downstream consumption of derived targets. Verification can be grounded in: (1) artifact inspection (performance target documents with source traceability), (2) workflow audit logs (review gate timestamps and approver records), (3) baseline history records (versioning and calibration change logs).
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: CLEAR. Safety Services is unambiguous owner of DP-C. Containment score 0.92 reflects strong domain fit. FR-to-IM path is direct: FR (derive performance requirements from worksite mission context) → DP-C (two-stage extraction + performance-mapping workflow) → Safety Services (centralized module with safety plan/hazard assessment access and review governance). No competing module claims. External interfaces (Tasking, SafetyAlert, Telemetry) are well-defined and directional, reducing ambiguity about responsibility boundaries.

diffusion_risks: TELEMETRY COUPLING RISK (Medium): Stage (1) mission context extraction may require real-time or near-real-time telemetry fusion (e.g., environmental conditions, operational state) to enrich worksite documentation. If Telemetry data is tightly coupled to Safety Services derivation logic, asynchronous delays or schema mismatches could block performance target generation. Mitigation: Define Telemetry interface as event-driven data feed with explicit schema versioning and fallback behavior when telemetry is unavailable; ensure Safety Services can operate on static documentation alone if needed.

TEMPLATE CUSTOMIZATION BOTTLENECK (Medium): Performance-mapping templates are assumed standardized, but worksite-specific variations (mining vs. construction vs. offshore) may require template proliferation. If Safety Services hard-codes templates, each new worksite type requires module modification, creating a diffusion point across multiple deployment contexts. Mitigation: Implement pluggable template registry pattern; templates are validated artifacts loaded at runtime, not compiled into Safety Services logic. Template validation remains Safety Services responsibility; template authoring can be distributed.

HUMAN-IN-THE-LOOP COORDINATION (Low-Medium): Manual review gates and sign-off checkpoints imply synchronous human decision points. If approvers are geographically distributed or operate under Teleoperation domain (remote operators), coordination latency and role ambiguity may arise. Current IM boundary does not explicitly model approval workflow ownership or escalation paths. Mitigation: Define approval workflow as explicit sub-process within Safety Services with role assignments (e.g., Safety Officer, Mission Lead) independent of Teleoperation module; use asynchronous notification pattern to avoid blocking Teleoperation operations.

WORKSITE DOCUMENTATION ACCESS (Low): Stage (1) assumes Safety Services has authoritative access to worksite safety plans, operational procedures, and hazard assessments. If these documents are managed by external systems (e.g., enterprise document management, field databases), Safety Services becomes dependent on external data sources. Diffusion risk if document retrieval logic is scattered across multiple interfaces. Mitigation: Establish SafetyAlert interface as single source of truth for worksite documentation ingestion; centralize document schema and validation within Safety Services.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: EXCELLENT CONTAINMENT. Safety Services module achieves 0.92 containment score with minimal diffusion (0.08). DP-C is fundamentally a safety-domain workflow: mission context extraction from safety artifacts (safety plans, hazard assessments, operational procedures) is native to Safety Services; performance-mapping templates and review gates are safety validation mechanisms; derived targets flow cleanly outbound to Tasking (mission planning) and SafetyAlert (constraint validation) without requiring bidirectional coupling or cross-module duplication. No competing modules (NGHE Solution, Vehicle, Teleoperation) have legitimate claims on DP-C logic. Outbound interfaces are unidirectional and well-scoped: Tasking receives derived targets; SafetyAlert receives validated constraints. Inbound Telemetry interface is optional enrichment, not core dependency. IM boundary is cohesive and defensible.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-C directly addresses FR-NGHE-005.1 requirement to derive performance requirements from worksite mission context. Two-stage workflow (extraction + performance-mapping) is logically sound and traceable: Stage (1) extracts mission context from worksite documentation (safety plan, operational procedures, hazard assessments)—directly sourcing from FR input domain. Stage (2) applies standardized performance-mapping template to convert context elements into measurable targets—directly producing FR output (performance requirements). Manual review gates and sign-off checkpoints satisfy implicit safety-critical governance requirement. DP-C is implementable and verifiable against acceptance criteria: documented performance targets with traceability to worksite inputs and maintained baseline history are natural outputs of the two-stage workflow with review gates.

recommended_revisions: REVISION 1 - Telemetry Interface Specification: Explicitly define Telemetry interface as asynchronous event-driven data feed with schema versioning and optional enrichment semantics. Document fallback behavior: Safety Services shall derive performance targets from static worksite documentation alone if Telemetry is unavailable or delayed. Add schema validation and error handling to prevent Telemetry schema drift from blocking Safety Services derivation logic.

REVISION 2 - Template Registry Pattern: Introduce pluggable template registry within Safety Services. Templates are validated artifacts (JSON/YAML) loaded at runtime, not hard-coded. Template validation (schema compliance, safety constraint coverage) remains Safety Services responsibility. Document template authoring process and approval workflow to enable worksite-specific customization without modifying core Safety Services code.

REVISION 3 - Approval Workflow Sub-Process: Decompose manual review gates into explicit approval workflow sub-process within Safety Services. Define roles (Safety Officer, Mission Lead, Worksite Manager) and escalation paths independent of Teleoperation module. Use asynchronous notification pattern (e.g., event-driven approval requests) to avoid blocking downstream Tasking or Teleoperation operations. Document approval SLA and timeout behavior.

REVISION 4 - Worksite Documentation Ingestion: Establish SafetyAlert interface as single authoritative source for worksite documentation (safety plans, operational procedures, hazard assessments). Define document schema and versioning strategy. Centralize document retrieval and validation logic within Safety Services to avoid diffusion across multiple data sources. Document fallback behavior if SafetyAlert is unavailable.

REVISION 5 - Traceability and Audit Trail: Explicitly specify that Safety Services maintains immutable audit trail of: (1) source worksite documentation (with version/timestamp), (2) performance-mapping template applied (with version), (3) derived performance targets (with derivation timestamp), (4) review gate approvals (with approver identity and timestamp), (5) baseline/calibration updates (with change reason and history). Audit trail shall be queryable and exportable for compliance verification.

REVISION 6 - Acceptance Criteria Mapping: Add explicit acceptance criteria verification plan: (A) Artifact inspection: performance target documents shall include source traceability (worksite input → template rule → derived target); (B) Workflow audit: review gate logs shall record all approvals with timestamps and approver identity; (C) Baseline history: Safety Services shall maintain versioned baseline records with calibration change logs; (D) Interface validation: Tasking and SafetyAlert shall receive derived targets and constraints within SLA; Telemetry interface shall not block derivation if unavailable.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with HIGH verification feasibility and EXCELLENT containment. However, the recommended revisions expose critical design gaps that must be resolved before proceeding to sub-FR decomposition. Specifically: (1) Telemetry coupling risk requires explicit interface specification and fallback semantics to prevent runtime blocking; (2) Template customization bottleneck demands pluggable registry pattern to avoid diffusion across worksite variants; (3) Approval workflow lacks explicit role/escalation modeling, creating coordination ambiguity in safety-critical context; (4) Worksite documentation ingestion needs single-source-of-truth specification to prevent data diffusion. These are not minor implementation details—they are architectural decisions that affect module cohesion, interface clarity, and safety governance. Revising DP-C and IM boundary now prevents downstream decomposition errors and ensures sub-FRs inherit a sound, defensible design. High safety criticality amplifies the cost of deferring these clarifications.

revise.why: DP-C (two-stage extraction + performance-mapping workflow) is logically sound but architecturally underspecified. Revisions must address: (1) Telemetry interface as asynchronous optional enrichment with schema versioning and fallback to static documentation; (2) Template registry as pluggable runtime artifact store with validation governance; (3) Approval workflow as explicit sub-process with role assignments and asynchronous notification pattern; (4) Worksite documentation ingestion via SafetyAlert as single authoritative source with schema and versioning. These revisions strengthen DP-C by making interface contracts, data dependencies, and governance boundaries explicit. IM (Safety Services) boundary remains appropriate, but its internal structure must clarify: (a) core derivation logic (extraction + mapping) vs. optional enrichment (Telemetry fusion); (b) template management subsystem (registry, validation, versioning); (c) approval workflow subsystem (role-based gates, escalation, audit trail); (d) documentation ingestion subsystem (SafetyAlert interface, schema validation, fallback behavior). Revised DP-C and IM will be ready for sub-FR decomposition once these subsystems are explicitly modeled.

assumptions: Telemetry data is optional enrichment; Safety Services can derive performance targets from static worksite documentation alone.
Worksite-specific template variations (mining vs. construction vs. offshore) are managed via pluggable registry, not hard-coded module logic.
Approval workflow roles (Safety Officer, Mission Lead, Worksite Manager) are defined independently of Teleoperation domain and operate asynchronously.
Worksite documentation (safety plans, operational procedures, hazard assessments) is authoritative source ingested via SafetyAlert interface.
Audit trail (source traceability, template versions, approvals, baseline history) is immutable and queryable for compliance verification.
Performance target derivation is deterministic given worksite documentation and template; human review gates validate correctness, not generate targets.
open_risks: TELEMETRY COUPLING RISK (Medium): Real-time telemetry fusion may introduce asynchronous delays or schema mismatches. Mitigation: Define Telemetry as event-driven optional feed with explicit fallback to static documentation.

TEMPLATE CUSTOMIZATION BOTTLENECK (Medium): Worksite-specific template proliferation may require module modification. Mitigation: Implement pluggable template registry with runtime loading and validation.

HUMAN-IN-THE-LOOP COORDINATION (Low-Medium): Distributed approvers and Teleoperation domain coordination may create latency and role ambiguity. Mitigation: Define approval workflow as explicit sub-process with asynchronous notification pattern.

WORKSITE DOCUMENTATION ACCESS (Low): External document management systems may scatter retrieval logic. Mitigation: Establish SafetyAlert as single authoritative source with centralized schema and validation.
APPROVAL SLA AND TIMEOUT BEHAVIOR (Low): Synchronous review gates may block downstream Tasking operations if approvers are unavailable. Mitigation: Define approval SLA and timeout escalation paths.

TEMPLATE SCHEMA DRIFT (Low): Template versioning and validation must prevent incompatible schema changes from breaking derivation logic. Mitigation: Implement schema versioning and backward compatibility checks.
evidence_needed: Telemetry interface specification: schema definition, versioning strategy, event-driven semantics, fallback behavior when unavailable.
Template registry design: pluggable architecture, runtime loading mechanism, validation rules, schema compliance checks, authoring process.
Approval workflow specification: role definitions (Safety Officer, Mission Lead, Worksite Manager), escalation paths, SLA targets, asynchronous notification pattern, timeout behavior.
Worksite documentation ingestion specification: SafetyAlert interface contract, document schema, versioning strategy, retrieval logic, validation rules, fallback behavior.
Audit trail specification: immutable record structure, queryable interface, exportable format, retention policy, compliance mapping.
Acceptance criteria verification plan: artifact inspection checklist, workflow audit log format, baseline history schema, interface validation test cases.
Safety governance mapping: how approval workflow integrates with safety-critical decision gates, role accountability, escalation authority, compliance audit trail.
